old_claim.note: Double-Entry Bookkeeping Diffusion provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Probability & Statistical Inference is the direct mathematical lineage for 19th-century regression, correlation, and analytical statistics; double-entry bookkeeping is only a distant data-recording context.

source_shape.source_locator: Introduction: says statistical activities are associated with probability models and that mathematical statistics designates the probability and statistical-inference theories used in statistical practice.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source frames probability and statistical inference as the mathematical theory used by modern statistical practice.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The corrected edge is about mathematical lineage for regression, correlation, and analytical statistics, while bookkeeping only supplies a distant record-keeping context.

ontology_before: Statistics (Advanced Data Analysis) depended on Double-Entry Bookkeeping Diffusion, overemphasizing commercial recordkeeping rather than the statistical-mathematical lineage.
ontology_after: Statistics (Advanced Data Analysis) depends on Probability & Statistical Inference alongside mathematics, scientific method, print, and logarithms/calculus context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: statistics_advanced_data_analysis now directly depends on probability_statistics_inference and no longer directly depends on double_entry_diffusion.
would_reject_if: The node is rescoped to accounting tables or commercial bookkeeping analytics rather than statistical methods.
A stronger source shows double-entry bookkeeping was a necessary prerequisite for 19th-century regression or correlation.
probability_statistics_inference is removed without replacing it with a narrower probability/statistical-theory predecessor.
short_reason: Advanced statistics descends from probability and statistical inference, not bookkeeping.
